MINI MINUTES FOR DEC-16-2013 09:30 AM FOR DEPT 302

LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANT PROPANE STUDIO LLC'S MOTION FOR LEAVE TO FILE CROSS-COMPLAINT IS GRANTED. CROSS-COMPLAINANTS SHALL FILE THE PROPOSED CROSS-COMPLAINT WITHIN 10 DAYS OF ENTRY OF THIS ORDER. THE COURT FINDS THE PROPOSED CROSS-COMPLAINT IS COMPULSORY AND THEREFORE GOVERNED BY CCP 426.50. CCP 426.50 HAS BEEN INTERPRETED AS REQUIRING THE COURT TO GRANT LEAVE TO FILE A COMPULSORY CROSS-COMPLAINT ABSENT BAD FAITH. (SILVER ORGANIZATIONS LTD. V. FRANK (1990) 217 CAL.APP.3D 94, 98-99.) THE LAW STRONGLY FAVORS GRANTING LEAVE, AS COMPULSORY CLAIMS ARE BARRED IF NOT PLED IN THE UNDERLYING CASE. (SIDNEY V. SUP. CT. (1988) 198 CAL.APP.3D 710, 718.) THE PRINCIPLE OF LIBERALITY IN CCP 426.50 "REQUIRES THAT A STRONG SHOWING OF BAD FAITH BE MADE IN ORDER TO SUPPORT A DENIAL OF THE RIGHT TO FILE A CROSS-COMPLAINT'[I]T IS PREFERABLE THAT THE PARTIES HAVE THEIR DAY IN COURT." (ID.) PLAINTIFFS HAVE NOT MET THE VERY HIGH BURDEN OF ESTABLISHING BAD FAITH HERE. DELAY IN FILING A CROSS-COMPLAINT IS NOT SUFFICIENT TO ESTABLISH BAD FAITH, UNLESS THE FILING OF THE CROSS-COMPLAINT WOULD "WORK A SUBSTANTIAL INJUSTICE TO THE OPPOSING PARTY AND WOULD PREJUDICE THE OPPOSING PARTY'S POSITION IN SOME WAY." (FOOT'S TRANSFER & STORAGE CO. V. SUPERIOR COURT. (1980) 114 CAL.APP.3D 897, 903.) THE EVIDENCE DOES NOT ESTABLISH SUBSTANTIAL INJUSTICE OR PREJUDICE HERE. (SEE E.G., GHERMAN V. COLBURN (1977) 72 CAL.APP.3D 544, WHERE THE COURT FOUND BAD FAITH BECAUSE THE EVIDENCE SUGGESTING THAT THE CROSS-COMPLAINANT'S PURPOSE FOR FILING THE CROSS-COMPLAINT WAS TO PREVENT A JURY TRIAL AMOUNTED TO SUBSTANTIAL INJUSTICE.) (PREVAILING PARTY TO PREPARE A FORM OF ORDER.) JUDGE: A. JAMES ROBERTSON II, CLERK: LESLEY FISCELLA, NOT REPORTED.

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To File Cross-Complaint
Off Calendar Dec-16-2013 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearance on this matter. RULING: The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT PROPANE STUDIO LLC'S Motion For Leave To File Cross-Complaint IS Granted. Cross-complainants shall file the proposed cross-complaint within 10 days of entry of this order. The Court finds the proposed cross-complaint is compulsory and therefore governed by CCP 426.50. CCP 426.50 has been interpreted as requiring the Court to grant leave to file a compulsory cross-complaint absent bad faith. (Silver Organizations Ltd. v. Frank (1990) 217 Cal.App.3d 94, 98-99.) The law strongly favors granting leave, as compulsory claims are barred if not pled in the underlying case. (Sidney v. Sup. Ct. (1988) 198 Cal.App.3d 710, 718.) The principle of liberality in CCP 426.50 "requires that a strong showing of bad faith be made in order to support a denial of the right to file a cross-complaint'[i]t is preferable that the parties have their day in court." (Id.) Plaintiffs have not met the very high burden of establishing bad faith here. Delay in filing a cross-complaint is not sufficient to establish bad faith, unless the filing of the cross-complaint would "work a substantial injustice to the opposing party and would prejudice the opposing party's position in some way." (Foot's Transfer & Storage Co. v. Superior Court. (1980) 114 Cal.App.3d 897, 903.) The evidence does not establish substantial injustice or prejudice here. (See e.g., Gherman v. Colburn (1977) 72 Cal.App.3d 544, where the court found bad faith because the evidence suggesting that the cross-complainant's purpose for filing the cross-complaint was to prevent a jury trial amounted to substantial injustice.) (Prevailing party to prepare a form of order.) Judge: A. James Robertson II, Clerk: Lesley Fiscella, Not reported.
